content.body: The photoelectric effect shows that light behaves as particles (photons). Einstein explained it in 1905: electrons are emitted when light above a threshold frequency hits a metal surface.

Photon energy: E = hf = hc/λ
h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant)

Photoelectric equation: hf = φ + ½mv²_max
where φ = work function (minimum energy to eject electron)

Threshold frequency: f₀ = φ/h

content.title: Photoelectric Calculation
content.body:
Metal has work function φ = 2.0 eV. Light of frequency 7×10¹⁴ Hz hits it.
h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ J·s, 1eV = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ J
hf = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ × 7×10¹⁴ = 4.64×10⁻¹⁹ J
φ = 2.0 × 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ = 3.2×10⁻¹⁹ J
KE_max = hf - φ = 1.44×10⁻¹⁹ J = 0.9 eV

questions.question: The photoelectric effect proves light has:
questions.explanation: The photoelectric effect cannot be explained by wave theory — photons are needed

questions.question: The work function is:
options: Minimum energy to eject an electron
questions.answer: Minimum energy to eject an electron
questions.explanation: Work function φ is the minimum energy needed to free an electron from the surface

questions.question: Doubling the frequency of light (above threshold) will:
questions.explanation: Higher frequency → more photon energy → greater KE_max = hf - φ

questions.question: Planck's constant h has units of:
questions.explanation: E = hf, so h = E/f = J/Hz = J·s

questions.question: Below the threshold frequency, increasing light intensity:
questions.explanation: Below threshold frequency, no individual photon has enough energy, regardless of intensity

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

content.body:
Planck's quantum theory:
Energy of a photon: E = hf = hc/λ
h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant)
f = frequency, c = 3×10⁸ m/s, λ = wavelength

Photoelectric effect (Einstein):
max KE of electron = hf − φ
φ = work function (minimum energy to release electron)
Threshold frequency f₀ = φ/h

The photoelectric effect proved light is quantized (photons). Key facts:
• Increasing intensity increases number of electrons, not their KE
• Increasing frequency increases electron KE
• Below threshold frequency, no electrons emitted regardless of intensity
• Emission is instantaneous — no delay

de Broglie wavelength (wave-particle duality):
λ = h/mv = h/p
All matter has wave properties
Electron diffraction proves electrons behave as waves

questions.question: Energy of a photon E =
questions.explanation: E = hf where h is Planck's constant and f is frequency.

questions.question: The work function φ is...
options: Minimum energy to release an electron from a surface
questions.answer: Minimum energy to release an electron from a surface
questions.explanation: The work function φ is the minimum energy needed to liberate an electron from the metal surface.

questions.question: Above threshold frequency, increasing light intensity...
questions.explanation: Greater intensity = more photons = more electrons. But each photon still gives the same energy, so KE is unchanged.

questions.question: Maximum KE of photoelectrons =
questions.explanation: Einstein: max KE = photon energy − work function = hf − φ

questions.question: No photoelectric effect occurs when...
questions.explanation: Below the threshold frequency, even very intense light cannot release electrons. Each photon has insufficient energy.

questions.question: Planck's constant h =
questions.explanation: Planck's constant h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ J·s (joule-seconds)

questions.question: de Broglie wavelength λ = h/p. A faster particle has...
questions.explanation: λ = h/p = h/mv. Greater momentum p → smaller wavelength. Fast particles have shorter de Broglie wavelengths.

questions.question: The photoelectric effect proved that light consists of...
questions.explanation: Einstein's explanation of the photoelectric effect required light to come in discrete energy packets called photons.

questions.question: Threshold frequency f₀ =
questions.explanation: At threshold: hf₀ = φ (just enough energy). So f₀ = φ/h.

questions.question: Electron diffraction demonstrates that electrons have...
questions.explanation: Electron diffraction (electrons passing through crystal lattice and forming interference patterns) proves electrons have wave properties.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Photoelectric effect:
E_photon = hf = hc/λ
h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ J⋅s (Planck's constant)

Work function φ: minimum energy to release electron
Maximum KE of emitted electron:
KE_max = hf - φ

Threshold frequency: f₀ = φ/h (minimum to cause emission)

Stopping potential V_s: eV_s = KE_max

de Broglie wavelength:
λ = h/p = h/(mv)

content.title: Photoelectric Calculation
content.body:
Metal: φ=3.2×10⁻¹⁹J, illuminated by f=8×10¹⁴Hz
E_photon = 6.63×10⁻³⁴×8×10¹⁴ = 5.3×10⁻¹⁹J
KE_max = 5.3×10⁻¹⁹ - 3.2×10⁻¹⁹ = 2.1×10⁻¹⁹J
Stopping potential = 2.1×10⁻¹⁹/1.6×10⁻¹⁹ = 1.31V

content.title: de Broglie
content.body:
Electron: m=9.11×10⁻³¹kg, v=2×10⁶m/s
λ=h/mv=6.63×10⁻³⁴/(9.11×10⁻³¹×2×10⁶)
=3.64×10⁻¹⁰m = 0.364nm
(comparable to atomic spacings → electron diffraction!)

questions.question: Photoelectric effect shows light behaves as
questions.explanation: Photoelectric effect: light arrives in discrete packets (photons) of energy E=hf — classical wave theory fails

questions.question: Energy of photon E =
questions.explanation: E=hf where h=Planck's constant and f=frequency

questions.question: Increasing intensity of light below threshold frequency
questions.explanation: Below threshold f₀: photons lack energy to overcome work function — NO photoelectric effect

questions.question: Work function φ represents
options: minimum energy to release electron from metal surface
questions.answer: minimum energy to release electron from metal surface
questions.explanation: Work function: binding energy of most loosely held surface electrons

questions.question: Stopping potential V_s measures
questions.explanation: Applied negative voltage that just stops fastest electrons: eV_s = KE_max = hf - φ

questions.question: de Broglie wavelength λ = h/p means
options: moving particles have associated wave properties
questions.answer: moving particles have associated wave properties
questions.explanation: de Broglie: matter has wave properties. λ=h/mv — significant only for tiny masses (electrons)

questions.question: Electron diffraction confirms
questions.explanation: Electrons diffracting through crystals produce interference patterns — wave behaviour of matter

questions.question: Heisenberg uncertainty principle: ΔxΔp ≥ ħ/2 means
options: position and momentum are exactly measurable
more precise position → less precise momentum (and vice versa)
questions.answer: more precise position → less precise momentum (and vice versa)
questions.explanation: Fundamental quantum limit: cannot simultaneously know exact position and momentum

questions.question: Photon of wavelength 500nm. Energy (h=6.63×10⁻³⁴, c=3×10⁸)
Wave-particle duality means
options: light and matter exhibit both wave AND particle properties depending on measurement
questions.answer: light and matter exhibit both wave AND particle properties depending on measurement
questions.explanation: Duality: light diffracts (wave) but causes photoelectric effect (particle). Electrons diffract (wave) but have mass (particle)

content.body:
Nuclear fission: heavy nucleus splits
²³⁵U + n → fission products + 2-3n + energy
Chain reaction: each fission releases neutrons
Binding energy released ≈ 200 MeV per fission

Nuclear fusion: light nuclei combine
²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV
Requires T~10⁸K (plasma)
Fusion releases more energy per kg than fission

Standard Model particles:
Quarks (up,down,strange,charm,bottom,top)
Leptons (electron,muon,tau + neutrinos)
Bosons (photon,W±,Z⁰,gluon,Higgs)

content.title: Fission Reactor
content.body:
Moderator (water/graphite): slows neutrons
Control rods (Boron): absorb neutrons
Coolant: transfers heat to turbine
Shielding: concrete reduces radiation
Critical mass: enough fissile material for sustained chain reaction

content.title: Quark Model
content.body:
Proton: uud (2 up + 1 down)
Neutron: udd (1 up + 2 down)
Mesons: quark-antiquark pairs
Charge: up=+2/3, down=-1/3
Proton: 2(2/3)+(-1/3) = 4/3-1/3 = +1 ✓

questions.question: Nuclear fission releases energy because
options: nuclear binding energy released when heavy nucleus splits into more stable fragments
questions.answer: nuclear binding energy released when heavy nucleus splits into more stable fragments
questions.explanation: Mass defect converted to energy E=mc²; fission products have higher binding energy per nucleon than U-235

questions.question: Chain reaction in fission requires
options: each fission releasing ≥1 neutron that triggers further fissions
questions.answer: each fission releasing ≥1 neutron that triggers further fissions
questions.explanation: Each ²³⁵U fission releases 2-3 neutrons. If ≥1 causes another fission → sustained chain reaction

questions.question: Control rods in nuclear reactor function to
options: absorb excess neutrons to control reaction rate
questions.answer: absorb excess neutrons to control reaction rate
questions.explanation: Boron or cadmium control rods: insert to slow reaction, withdraw to speed up

questions.question: Fusion requires very high temperature because
options: protons must overcome electrostatic repulsion (Coulomb barrier) to fuse
questions.answer: protons must overcome electrostatic repulsion (Coulomb barrier) to fuse
questions.explanation: ~10⁸K needed for kinetic energy to overcome repulsion between positively charged nuclei

questions.question: Fusion produces more energy per kg than fission because
options: fusion reaction energy/mass ratio is higher (smaller nuclei combine to much more stable He)
questions.answer: fusion reaction energy/mass ratio is higher (smaller nuclei combine to much more stable He)
questions.explanation: H→He: massive increase in binding energy per nucleon. Sun produces 4×10²⁶W by fusion

questions.question: Proton is made of quarks
questions.explanation: Proton: 2 up quarks + 1 down quark. Charge: 2(+2/3)+1(-1/3)=+1

questions.question: Neutron is made of quarks
questions.explanation: Neutron: 1 up + 2 down. Charge: 1(+2/3)+2(-1/3)=0

questions.question: Which is NOT a lepton?
questions.explanation: Leptons: electron, muon, tau and their neutrinos. Quarks are a separate family of fundamental particles

questions.question: Photon is the force carrier of
questions.explanation: Photon: boson mediating electromagnetic interaction. Gluon→strong, W/Z→weak, graviton(hypothetical)→gravity

questions.question: Higgs boson discovery (2012) confirmed
options: existence of mass-giving field (Higgs field) that gives particles mass
questions.answer: existence of mass-giving field (Higgs field) that gives particles mass
questions.explanation: Higgs boson: excitation of Higgs field. Particles gain mass by interacting with this field

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
De Broglie wavelength:
λ = h/p = h/(mv)
All matter has wave properties
Wavelength significant only for tiny particles (small m, large h/p)

Electron diffraction confirms wave nature
Photon momentum: p = h/λ = E/c

Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle:
Δx⋅Δp ≥ h/(4π)
ΔE⋅Δt ≥ h/(4π)
(cannot simultaneously know position and momentum precisely)

content.title: De Broglie Examples
content.body:
Electron at 10⁶ m/s:
λ = 6.63×10⁻³⁴/(9.11×10⁻³¹×10⁶) = 7.28×10⁻¹⁰ m
(similar to atomic spacing → diffraction observed!)

Baseball (0.15kg, 40m/s):
λ = 6.63×10⁻³⁴/(0.15×40) = 1.1×10⁻³⁴ m
(unmeasurably small → no wave effects)

content.title: Young's Double Slit with Electrons
content.body:
Electrons sent one at a time through double slits
Build up interference pattern over time
Each electron interferes with itself (wave)
Observing which slit → pattern disappears (particle)
Observer effect: measurement disturbs quantum system

questions.question: De Broglie wavelength λ=h/p. Doubling momentum of a particle
questions.explanation: λ=h/p: if p doubles, λ=h/2p → wavelength halves

questions.question: Electron diffraction confirms electrons behave as
questions.explanation: Diffraction is a wave phenomenon. Electrons producing diffraction patterns proves their wave nature

questions.question: De Broglie wavelength of macroscopic objects is immeasurably small because
options: h is small and mv is large → λ=h/mv extremely small
questions.answer: h is small and mv is large → λ=h/mv extremely small
questions.explanation: h≈6.6×10⁻³⁴ is tiny. For 1g ball at 1m/s: λ≈10⁻³¹m — impossible to detect

questions.question: Heisenberg uncertainty principle says we cannot simultaneously know precisely
questions.explanation: ΔxΔp ≥ h/4π: more precisely we know position, less precisely we can know momentum, and vice versa

questions.question: Quantum tunnelling occurs because
options: wave function extends into classically forbidden regions (uncertainty in position)
questions.answer: wave function extends into classically forbidden regions (uncertainty in position)
questions.explanation: Tunnelling: particle's probability wave extends through barrier — fundamental to fusion, transistors, scanning tunneling microscope

questions.question: Photon with wavelength 500nm has momentum
Wave-particle duality means
options: electrons and photons exhibit both wave and particle behaviours depending on experiment
questions.answer: electrons and photons exhibit both wave and particle behaviours depending on experiment
questions.explanation: Quantum objects show wave behaviour (diffraction, interference) and particle behaviour (photoelectric, Compton) — not simultaneously

questions.question: Observer effect in quantum mechanics means
options: measuring a system inevitably disturbs it due to quantum nature
questions.answer: measuring a system inevitably disturbs it due to quantum nature
questions.explanation: To observe a quantum particle, you must interact with it (e.g. with a photon) which changes its state

questions.question: Scanning tunneling microscope works by
options: quantum tunneling current between tip and surface to image individual atoms
questions.answer: quantum tunneling current between tip and surface to image individual atoms
questions.explanation: STM: electrons tunnel between sharp metal tip and surface; tunnel current extremely sensitive to distance → atomic resolution imaging

questions.question: Uncertainty Principle: ΔE⋅Δt ≥ h/4π means
options: energy and time can be exactly known
very short-lived particles can have large uncertainty in energy (mass/width)
questions.answer: very short-lived particles can have large uncertainty in energy (mass/width)
questions.explanation: Unstable particles exist briefly (small Δt) → large ΔE → measured mass has spread (natural linewidth/resonance width)

questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

content.body:
LASER: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation
Population inversion: more atoms in excited state than ground state
Stimulated emission: photon triggers atom to emit identical photon (same phase, direction, frequency)
Coherent light: all waves in phase

Solid state energy bands:
Conductors: overlapping bands
Semiconductors: small band gap (Si: 1.1eV)
Insulators: large band gap (>5eV)
Conduction band / Valence band / Band gap

content.title: Laser Operation
content.body:
3-level laser:
1. Pump: atoms excited to E₂
2. Fast decay to metastable E₁
3. Stimulated emission at E₁→E₀
Population inversion between E₁ and E₀

Applications: CD/DVD/Blu-ray readers, barcode scanners, surgery, fibre optics communications, laser cutting, range finding

content.title: Semiconductors in Detail
content.body:
Intrinsic semiconductor (pure Si): few carriers at room temperature
Doping: n-type (phosphorus: extra electron), p-type (boron: hole)
p-n junction: depletion region, forward/reverse bias
Solar cell: photons create electron-hole pairs → current
Band gap of LED determines colour of light emitted

questions.question: LASER stands for
options: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation
questions.answer: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation
questions.explanation: LASER acronym: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation

questions.question: Population inversion means
options: more atoms in excited state than ground state
questions.answer: more atoms in excited state than ground state
questions.explanation: Population inversion: necessary condition for laser operation — more atoms ready to emit than absorb

questions.question: Stimulated emission produces photon
options: identical to triggering photon (same phase, frequency, direction)
questions.answer: identical to triggering photon (same phase, frequency, direction)
questions.explanation: Stimulated emission: incident photon triggers emission of identical photon → coherent, directed beam

questions.question: Laser light is coherent meaning
options: all photons in same phase and direction
questions.answer: all photons in same phase and direction
questions.explanation: Coherence: fixed phase relationship between all photons — enables interference, holography, precise measurements

questions.question: Band gap in semiconductors is
options: energy gap between valence and conduction bands
questions.answer: energy gap between valence and conduction bands
questions.explanation: Band gap: electrons must gain this minimum energy to jump from valence to conduction band and conduct electricity

questions.question: p-type semiconductor has
questions.explanation: p-type: doped with acceptor (e.g. boron, 3 valence electrons). Creates holes as majority carriers

questions.question: n-type semiconductor has
questions.explanation: n-type: doped with donor (e.g. phosphorus, 5 valence electrons). Extra electron per dopant atom

questions.question: Solar cell converts
options: light to electrical energy via photovoltaic effect
questions.answer: light to electrical energy via photovoltaic effect
questions.explanation: Solar cell: photons absorbed at p-n junction create electron-hole pairs → separated by electric field → current

questions.question: LED colour depends on
options: semiconductor band gap (photon energy = band gap energy)
questions.answer: semiconductor band gap (photon energy = band gap energy)
questions.explanation: LED: electrons fall from conduction to valence band; energy released as photon with E=hf=band gap energy. Larger gap = higher f = bluer light

questions.question: Which application requires laser coherence specifically?
questions.explanation: Holography requires interference of light — only possible with coherent (same phase) laser light
